title: Latency: The Speed of Decisions
concept: Latency is the time it takes a served model to return a prediction after receiving a request — a critical factor for real-time systems, where complex models are often slower than simple ones.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why might a company still choose batch predictions over real-time, even though real-time is instant?

options.text: Batch processing is often cheaper and more efficient for tasks that don't need instant results.
Batch predictions are always more accurate than real-time ones.
Real-time predictions are illegal in most industries.

exercises.explain: Correct! Batch is often more cost-efficient when instant results aren't strictly necessary.

cards.title: The difference between a sports car and a freight truck
cards.body: A sports car can respond to a single passenger's request in seconds, but can't move a warehouse of goods. A freight truck can carry massive loads efficiently, but takes much longer to get moving. Machine learning models face a similar speed-versus-complexity tradeoff.

cards.title: Latency: The response clock
cards.body: Latency is the amount of time between a served model receiving a request and returning its prediction. For real-time applications, keeping latency extremely low (often milliseconds) is critical for a smooth user experience.

cards.title: The coffee shop line
cards.body: A simple linear regression model is like a coffee shop with one quick barista pouring pre-made drip coffee — very fast per customer. A large Random Forest with hundreds of trees is like a shop that hand-crafts each custom latte from scratch — higher quality perhaps, but each order takes noticeably longer.

cards.title: Latency in a game
cards.body: A video game's anti-cheat model needs to flag suspicious behavior within milliseconds to feel responsive, so teams often choose a fast, simple model even if a slower, more complex ensemble might squeeze out slightly higher accuracy — the tradeoff favors speed.

cards.title: The most accurate model isn't always the right choice
cards.body: You might assume you should always deploy whichever model scored highest in testing. In real-time systems, a slightly less accurate but much faster model is often the better business decision, since excessive latency can ruin the user experience or even violate strict response-time requirements.

cards.title: Latency Tradeoffs
cards.body: Speed vs. complexity in model choice:

left.text:
e.g. Logistic Regression.
Very fast to compute.
Ideal for strict real-time needs.

right.text:
e.g. Large Random Forest.
Slower per prediction.
Better suited to batch or relaxed timing.

exercises.prompt: What is latency in the context of model serving?

options.text: The time between a model receiving a request and returning a prediction.
The total size of the model file on disk.
The number of features used during training.
The accuracy score of the model on the test set.

exercises.explain: Correct! Latency measures response time, not accuracy or file size.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A large, complex ensemble model like a Random Forest with hundreds of trees typically has lower latency than a simple linear model.
exercises.explain: False! Complex ensemble models generally take longer to compute a prediction, giving them higher latency than simple models.

exercises.sentence: For strict real-time systems, teams often prefer a faster, simpler model over a slightly more accurate one to keep ___ low.
exercises.explain: Keeping latency low is often a top priority for real-time systems, sometimes even over raw accuracy.

exercises.prompt: A team must choose between Model A (99% accurate, 500ms response time) and Model B (96% accurate, 20ms response time) for a live video game's instant reaction system. Which factor makes Model B a strong contender despite lower accuracy?

options.text: Its much lower latency (20ms) is critical for a real-time system where instant response is essential to the user experience.
Model B automatically becomes 99% accurate once deployed.
Latency has no relevance to real-time gaming systems.

exercises.explain: Correct! In strict real-time contexts, the dramatically lower latency can outweigh a small accuracy gap.

exercises.prompt: A ride-sharing app's live pricing model must respond within 100 milliseconds or the app feels broken to users. Testing shows a complex ensemble model is 2% more accurate but takes 800 milliseconds per prediction, while a simpler model takes only 40 milliseconds. Which should the team deploy for this real-time feature, and why?

options.text: The simpler, faster model, since it comfortably meets the 100ms latency requirement, while the more accurate model would make the app feel broken.
The complex ensemble model, since higher accuracy should always be the only deciding factor.
Neither model, since latency doesn't matter for pricing decisions.
Both models simultaneously, averaged together, ignoring the timing requirement.

exercises.explain: Exactly! When a strict real-time requirement exists, latency becomes a hard constraint — a model that violates it is unusable regardless of its accuracy advantage. Next, let's look at a subtle danger that emerges once a model starts making live decisions: the feedback loop!
